children.name: Pachycephala
children.description: The typical whistlers — a large genus of stocky, big-headed passerines known for their extraordinarily loud, clear, melodic whistles that carry long distances through the forest. They are found from Southeast Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. Despite their small size, they have disproportionately large, rounded heads, which gives them their scientific name meaning 'thick head'.

children.name: Pachycephala pectoralis
children.description: A stunning whistler with the male a brilliant yellow below, white throat, black head band, and olive-green back, found from eastern Australia through Indonesia to the Pacific islands. Its rich, carrying whistle is one of the finest bird songs in the Australasian region. It forages in the canopy and understorey for insects, fruit, and seeds.

children.name: Pachycephala rufiventris
children.description: A medium-sized whistler with the male a warm grey above, rufous underparts, and a white throat, widespread across Australia, New Guinea, and New Caledonia. Its song is an explosive, ringing combination of whistles and trills that is one of the most characteristic sounds of Australian woodlands. It is a highly vocal, aggressive defender of its territory.

children.name: Pachycephala simplex
children.description: A plain, greyish-brown whistler with a pale throat and a simple, three-noted whistled song, found in lowland rainforests of northern Australia and New Guinea. Unlike most whistlers, the sexes are similar in plumage, both lacking any bright colouration. It forages in the mid-canopy, gleaning insects from leaves and branches.

children.name: Pachycephala olivacea
children.description: A largely olive-green and grey whistler with a white throat streaked with grey, found in the cool temperate rainforests and wet eucalypt forests of southeastern Australia. It is a shy, secretive bird that keeps to the dense understorey, unlike its more conspicuous relatives. Its beautiful, melancholy song consists of a series of clear, descending whistles.

children.name: Pachycephala melanura
children.description: A whistler closely resembling the Golden Whistler but with a black tail and more restricted yellow underparts, found in mangroves and coastal forests of northern Australia and New Guinea. It is closely associated with mangrove habitats. Its song is a loud, clear whistle.

children.name: Pachycephala citreogaster
children.description: The Bismarck whistler is a striking yellow-and-black whistler endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea. Males have a black head, yellow underparts, and olive-green upperparts. It inhabits lowland and hill forests on the islands.

children.name: Pachycephala macrorhyncha
children.description: The yellow-throated whistler has a distinctive yellow throat, white underparts, and olive-brown upperparts. It is found across the islands of Wallacea and the Lesser Sundas in Indonesia. Its song is a series of clear, ringing whistles.

children.name: Pachycephala soror
children.description: Sclater's whistler is a drab olive-brown whistler with a pale throat and a faint yellowish tinge on the belly, found in the mountains of New Guinea. It inhabits montane forests where it forages in the understory. Its song is a series of high-pitched whistles.

children.name: Pachycephala phaionota
children.description: The island whistler is a greyish-brown whistler with a pale throat and a faint yellowish wash on the underparts, endemic to the Moluccan islands of Indonesia. It inhabits lowland forests and secondary growth. Its song is a series of clear, musical notes.

children.name: Pachycephala fulvotincta
children.description: The rusty-breasted whistler has a distinctive rusty-orange breast band, grey upperparts, and a white throat. It is found in the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia. It inhabits forests and woodlands, foraging in the mid-canopy.

children.name: Pachycephala leucogastra
children.description: The white-bellied whistler has grey upperparts, a white belly, and a distinctive black band across the chest. It is found in the forests of New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago. Its song is a loud, far-carrying whistle.

children.name: Pachycephala mentalis
children.description: The black-chinned whistler has a distinctive black chin and throat, grey upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the Moluccan islands of Indonesia. It inhabits lowland forests and secondary growth. Its song is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Pachycephala griseonota
children.description: The drab whistler is a plain greyish-brown bird with a paler belly and throat, found in the lowland forests of the Moluccas and New Guinea. It is one of the least colourful whistlers. Its song is a series of simple, whistled notes.

children.name: Pachycephala lorentzi
children.description: Lorentz's whistler is a small, olive-brown whistler with a pale throat and yellowish underparts, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It is named after the Dutch explorer Hendrikus Lorentz. It forages in the understory, gleaning insects from foliage.

children.name: Pachycephala balim
children.description: The Baliem whistler is a recently described species from the Baliem Valley of Western New Guinea. It has olive-brown upperparts, a yellow throat, and white underparts. It inhabits montane forests at mid to high elevations.

children.name: Pachycephala schlegelii
children.description: The regent whistler is a striking black-and-yellow whistler found in the montane forests of New Guinea. Males have a black head and upperparts, a bright yellow belly, and a white throat. Its song is a loud, ringing whistle.
